section.title: Make Haste to Help Me
section.sectionRationale: A brief, urgent cry for immediate rescue, this psalm is nearly identical to the closing verses of Psalm 40, reused here as its own short prayer 'to bring remembrance,' teaching that God's people may return again and again to the same honest, urgent plea, 'make haste, O God, to deliver me,' without needing new words for every fresh crisis.
section.thesis: David's brief, urgent cry, 'hurry, O Lord, to help me,' models unashamed dependence and urgency in prayer, content to be 'poor and needy' before a God who does not delay, a pattern of honest, immediate appeal that anticipates every believer's confident access to God through Christ.
section.frameNote: Two movements: the urgent cry for God's help against those who seek his harm, verses 1-3; and the wish for the joy of those who love God's salvation and the honest confession of his own poverty and need, verses 4-5.

units.label: Make haste, O God, to deliver me
units.note: the urgent cry against those who seek his harm

verses.text: Make haste, O God, to deliver me! Hurry, O Lord, to help me!
May those who seek my life be ashamed and confounded; may those who wish me harm be repelled and {{kalam|humiliated}}.
May those who say, "Aha, aha!" retreat because of their shame.

units.label: I am poor and needy
units.note: joy for those who love God's salvation, honest confession of need

verses.text: May all who seek You rejoice and be glad in You; may those who love Your salvation always say, "Let God be magnified!"
But I am {{ani|poor}} and needy; hurry to me, O God. You are my help and my deliverer; O Lord, do not delay.

kalam.gloss: To humiliate, dishonor, or put to shame. David prays this specifically on those who mock and seek his harm, asking that their scheming against him be publicly reversed and exposed rather than succeed.

ani.gloss: Poor, afflicted, needy, often describing someone who has no resources of their own and depends entirely on another. David applies this word to himself even as reigning king, an honest confession of complete dependence on God rather than on his own position or strength.

basic.context: This short psalm is almost identical to the closing verses of Psalm 40, reused here as its own brief, urgent prayer. David cries out for immediate help against those who mock and seek his harm, and closes by honestly naming himself poor and needy before God.
basic.hermeneutics: The psalm shows that urgent, repeated prayer is not a lack of faith; David reuses nearly the same words from an earlier psalm, teaching that believers do not need fresh, original language for every new crisis, only honest, urgent dependence on God.
basic.originalLanguages: David prays that his mockers be kalam, humiliated, exposed and reversed in their scheming. And he confesses himself ani, poor and needy, even as reigning king, an honest admission of complete dependence on God rather than his own resources.
basic.typology: David's honest confession, 'I am poor and needy,' even while king, anticipates Christ, who though rich, for our sake became poor, 2 Corinthians 8:9, taking on the very posture of complete dependence and need that this psalm confesses.
basic.christ: Christ embodies this psalm's posture perfectly, though the eternal Son and rightful King, He emptied Himself and took the form of a servant, Philippians 2:7, becoming poor for our sake so that, in Him, we might become rich, 2 Corinthians 8:9, the ultimate fulfillment of the psalm's honest confession of need.
basic.god: God is shown as one who is called to hurry, to act without delay for those who are truly poor and needy, sovereign over the shame and reversal of those who scheme against His people, and worthy of being magnified by all who love His salvation.
basic.crossReferences: Psalm 40:13-17 supplies the nearly identical source text this psalm reuses as its own urgent prayer. 2 Corinthians 8:9 applies the language of voluntary poverty to Christ's incarnation for our sake. Philippians 4:6 echoes this psalm's pattern of urgent, honest prayer brought directly to God without delay.
basic.oneStory: David's honest, urgent cry as one who is poor and needy, repeated nearly word for word from an earlier psalm, anticipates Christ, who became poor for our sake so that all who are truly needy before God might find in Him a help that does not delay.

context.literary: A brief, two-part psalm of urgent petition, moving from a cry against those who mock and seek harm, verses 1-3, to a wish for the joy of God's true seekers and an honest confession of personal poverty and need, verses 4-5.
context.historical: Its content and superscription, 'to bring remembrance,' along with its near-identical match to Psalm 40:13-17, suggest it was deliberately extracted and reused as a short, standalone prayer for urgent occasions, a portable cry for help drawn from a longer psalm of thanksgiving.
context.canonical: Its close relationship to Psalm 40 shows how the Psalter itself modeled reusing and adapting earlier prayers for new, pressing occasions, rather than requiring wholly new composition for every fresh need.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To provide a short, urgent, reusable cry for help in moments of pressing crisis, modeling honest confession of need and confident appeal for God's immediate action.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes David's specific urgent plea against those who sought his harm; it prescribes as an abiding pattern honest, urgent prayer that does not hesitate to ask God to act without delay, and honest confession of one's own poverty and need before Him.

debates.question: Why does this psalm repeat, almost word for word, the closing verses of Psalm 40 rather than offering new material?

views.label: The repetition shows this psalm was deliberately extracted from Psalm 40 to function as a short, portable prayer for urgent occasions, valued precisely because it was already familiar and trusted, not because new composition was unavailable.
views.case: The distinctive addition, 'to bring remembrance,' in the superscription suggests a specific liturgical purpose for reusing this material as its own short prayer, rather than the psalm being an accidental duplicate.

views.label: The repetition demonstrates that urgent, heartfelt prayer does not require novelty; the same words, honestly prayed again in a new crisis, are just as acceptable to God as freshly composed language.
views.case: Nothing in Scripture suggests repeated or reused prayer language is inferior to invented language, David himself models praying essentially the same urgent cry more than once across different moments of need.

debates.bearing: Both readings affirm that reusing established, trusted prayer language is a legitimate and even valuable practice, encouraging believers today to return to familiar biblical prayers, including the Psalms themselves, rather than feeling pressure to always find new words for urgent need.

study.originalLanguages: David prays that his mockers be kalam, humiliated, their scheming publicly exposed and reversed. And he confesses himself ani, poor and needy, even as reigning king, verse 5, an honest admission of complete dependence on God rather than his own resources or position.

typology.type: David's honest confession, 'I am poor and needy,' even while king, verse 5
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates Christ, who though rich, for our sake became poor, 2 Corinthians 8:9, taking on the very posture of need this psalm confesses.

study.christ: Christ embodies this psalm's posture perfectly. Though the eternal Son and rightful King, He emptied Himself and took the form of a servant, Philippians 2:7, becoming poor for our sake so that, in Him, we might become rich, 2 Corinthians 8:9, the ultimate fulfillment of the psalm's honest confession, 'I am poor and needy.'
study.god: God is shown as one who is called to hurry, to act without delay for those who are truly poor and needy, sovereign over the shame and reversal of those who scheme against His people, and worthy of being magnified by all who love His salvation.

crossReferences.note: Supplies the nearly identical source text this psalm reuses as its own short, urgent prayer.
Applies the language of voluntary poverty for another's sake to Christ's incarnation.
Echoes this psalm's pattern of urgent, honest prayer brought directly to God without delay.

study.oneStory: David's honest, urgent cry as one who is poor and needy, repeated nearly word for word from an earlier psalm, anticipates Christ, who became poor for our sake so that all who are truly needy before God might find in Him a help that does not delay.

quiz.q: What does David ask God to do without delay, in verse 1?

options.text: Deliver him and hurry to help him.
options.note: The psalm opens with an urgent double request for immediate rescue.

options.text: Wait patiently until circumstances improve on their own.
options.note: The psalm's request is the opposite, urgent and immediate help, not patient waiting.

options.text: Give him wealth and status.
options.note: The request is for rescue and help, not material gain.

options.text: Send him a sign before acting.
options.note: David asks directly for action, not merely a preliminary sign.

quiz.q: What does David wish for those who love God's salvation, in verse 4?

options.text: That they would rejoice and be glad, saying 'Let God be magnified!'
options.note: This wish contrasts sharply with David's prayer against those who mock and seek his harm.

options.text: That they would remain silent and withdrawn.
options.note: The verse specifically wishes joy and vocal praise, not silence.

options.text: That they would gain political power over others.
options.note: The wish given is joy and praise of God, not political gain.

options.text: That they would receive material riches.
options.note: The specific wish is joy and the exclamation of God's magnification, not material wealth.

quiz.q: How does David describe himself in verse 5, even though he is king?

options.text: Poor and needy.
options.note: This honest confession of complete dependence on God stands even from a reigning king.

options.text: Wealthy and self-sufficient.
options.note: David explicitly names himself poor and needy, the opposite of self-sufficiency.

options.text: Powerful and unshakeable.
options.note: The confession given is one of need and dependence, not unshakeable power.

options.text: Wise beyond all his counselors.
options.note: The specific self-description is poverty and need, not superior wisdom.

quiz.q: This psalm is nearly identical to which other passage in the Psalter?

options.text: The closing verses of Psalm 40.
options.note: Psalm 70 reuses Psalm 40:13-17 nearly word for word as its own short, urgent prayer.

options.text: The opening verses of Psalm 1.
options.note: Psalm 70's parallel passage is the ending of Psalm 40, not the opening of Psalm 1.

options.text: The middle section of Psalm 119.
options.note: The parallel text is specifically Psalm 40:13-17, not any part of Psalm 119.

options.text: The entirety of Psalm 23.
options.note: The near-identical parallel is Psalm 40's closing verses, not Psalm 23.

quiz.q: How does the New Testament apply this psalm's honest confession of poverty and need to Christ?

options.text: Christ, though rich, became poor for our sake so that we might become rich, 2 Corinthians 8:9.
options.note: This directly parallels the psalm's posture of voluntary, self-emptying dependence and need.

options.text: The New Testament teaches Christ was never in need of anything at any point.
options.note: 2 Corinthians 8:9 and Philippians 2:7 both describe Christ taking on real, voluntary poverty and the form of a servant.

options.text: The New Testament has no connection between poverty and Christ's mission.
options.note: 2 Corinthians 8:9 explicitly connects Christ's becoming poor to the believer's spiritual enrichment.

options.text: Christ's poverty in the New Testament refers only to His lack of physical possessions.
options.note: The New Testament's language in 2 Corinthians 8:9 and Philippians 2:7 refers to Christ's whole self-emptying incarnation, not merely material possessions.

questions: David prays urgently, 'make haste... hurry,' without hesitation or embarrassment. Is there an urgent need in your life right now that you have been hesitant to bring honestly and immediately to God?

This psalm reuses, nearly word for word, an earlier psalm's closing prayer. What familiar prayer, whether from Scripture or your own past, could you return to honestly today rather than searching for new words?
David wishes joy specifically for 'those who love Your salvation,' verse 4. What would it look like for your own love of God's salvation to translate into visible joy today?

David confesses himself 'poor and needy' even as reigning king, verse 5. Where are you tempted to hide your own neediness behind competence, position, or achievement rather than confessing it honestly to God?
Christ became poor so that, in Him, we might become rich. How does reflecting on Christ's voluntary poverty for your sake change how you view your own present need or lack?

translationNotes.note: This psalm closely parallels Psalm 40:13-17, evidence that it was deliberately reused, likely for congregational or personal use as a short, urgent prayer, 'to bring remembrance,' per its superscription.
Humiliated renders kalam, a public reversal of shame, praying that the schemes of those seeking harm would themselves be exposed and turned back.
Poor and needy renders ani, describing someone with no resources of their own; David applies this humbling term to himself even while reigning as king.

meta.notes: BSB text retrieved via bible.com (YouVersion, Berean Standard Bible, version 3034) after the local makor-scripture toolkit failed with a persistent disk I/O error reading the sandbox-mounted database. Verse numbering and count (5 verses) verified against Psalm 70:1-70:5 per PSALMS-SECTION-MAP.md. The musical superscription is not rendered as verse text, consistent with the established convention in this corpus. No em or en dashes present. A John Piper message, 'In the Pits with a King,' was found and quotes this psalm at length, but its primary preached text is Psalm 40:1-3, not Psalm 70 itself, so it was not used as this psalm's verified sermon; sermon status is none.
